channel.title: A Founder's Story
channel.description: Founder-life photos, videos, route notes, guided-tour updates, and product stories from Philly Tours.

item.title: Marian Anderson, public stages, and why rec centers matter
item.description: In 1939, Marian Anderson turned a denial into a national civic lesson: public spaces can become public stages. In 2026 Philadelphia, debates about arts funding and rec-center reinvestment ask the same question—who gets a place to be heard, to learn, and to belong?

item.title: Overbrook High School and the politics of school buildings
item.description: Overbrook’s legends weren’t just made in a gym—they were made in a public institution. Philadelphia’s 2026 facilities master plan asks a hard civic question: what do we owe the neighborhood schools that create our shared life?

item.title: The Sonny Hill League and the Civic Infrastructure Under Our Feet
item.description: Philadelphia’s most famous summer basketball league wasn’t just about jump shots. It was a civic project—built on public courts, public trust, and the everyday budgets that decide whether neighborhood spaces stay open, safe, and cared for.

item.title: Du Bois, 'The Philadelphia Negro,' and What Eviction Data Demands Today
item.description: In 1899, W.E.B. Du Bois used Philadelphia as a laboratory for public-policy truth. In 2026, the city’s eviction-prevention systems show why housing data still has to be paired with power and due process.

item.title: The Free African Society and What Public Health Still Needs in a Heat Emergency
item.description: In 1793, Black Philadelphians organized mutual aid that helped keep the city alive. Philadelphia’s modern heat-emergency response shows why neighborhood trust and logistics still matter.

item.title: Garrett Morgan’s 1923 Traffic Signal and What “Vision Zero” Requires Now
item.description: A Black inventor’s three-position traffic signal was a small piece of civic infrastructure with a big idea—cities can design safety into everyday life. Philadelphia’s Vision Zero work shows how hard (and necessary) that idea still is.

item.title: Cecil B. Moore and the Question Behind Voting Rules Today
item.description: A North Philadelphia civil rights leader helps frame a current national debate: who gets easy access to the ballot, and who has to fight for it?
